source: Agent abee334 - Golf ball pain/solutions/hope quotes

quotes.quote: I enjoy hitting prov1s but I'm awful at following the ball from the tee and while my accuracy has gotten better I still hate losing balls that are quite expensive.
At $55/dozen, it hurts to lose a Pro V1. That's why golf continues to see new brands jumping into the direct-to-consumer, 'Pro V1 performance at a lower price' game.
I could never justify playing them. I could easily lose 3 or 4 balls a round at my old course, so the cost, and my lack of talent combined to make them way too expensive.
Only if you're happy with the cost of each ball you lose. I still lose a few balls every round so it's not worth it for me to head into the premium bracket.
The likes of AD333 and Supersofts are under half the price of ProV1s, which is a crazy difference. I'm not sure the ball is that good...
Not at all, it's a waste of money. At your level, the ball doesn't make any difference. It probably doesn't matter too much at my level either.
Been there, done that. Switched to used golf balls and never looked back. No more angst about $4 down the drain for a lost ball.
I lost three of them today which is very odd for me and I was so sad lol.
I've switched from TP5's to Snell MTB black, half the price and I noticed no difference at all. I like the pro V but until I'm on tour, I won't be wasting my money on them.
Love Pro V1s, but would never ever buy them. Fortunately I have PPs who give them to me when they find them.
I promise you nothing feels worse than watching a $2+ ball go in the drink.
If you can watch a $4 golf ball hit the drink and not worry, then ProV's won't hurt your game. Of course that line is blurred as well with the availability of slightly used premium balls.
Play with a golf ball you can afford to lose. If you can deal with losing $20 - $40 in balls per round, then play the Pro V1.
It isn't going to hurt your game but it WILL hurt your wallet. Instead of spending 55$ for a box of prov1's, go spend 20$ and use the other money for lessons.
Losing expensive balls, even if you can afford it just adds to the frustration factor when learning.
I believe the Pro V1 had too much spin for my game and was only hurting it.
That's one thing I did not even notice a difference. I scrambled on 3 holes with short chips and noticed no difference.. same check and release I got with a Pro V1.

And spin isn't good for everyone's game. For quite a few the spin hurts in all phases of the game including the short game where it makes shots less predictable for those who don't have consistent contact.
Golf Digest gets paid for advertising balls, it is in their interest that people buy more expensive balls and lose them. The logic behind that article is stupid.
Beginners are rarely able to spin the ball in any way. Their problems are not lack of spin with the wedges, but rather hitting the ball clean, hitting the green, hitting the fairway etc.
I switched from Pro V1's to Legato and couldn't be happier. They're billed as the equivalent and they weren't wrong and half the price.
I recently switched from Pro v1 to the more affordable Wilson Duo Optix and can't say enough good things about them.
I switched from Pro-V1's to the My Golf MG-1 Golf Balls after 35 years of Titleists variants. It's the best urethane ball at any price.
I played the Snell Black for years and found no difference between the Snell Black and a ProV1 except the price.
A premium golf ball can cost up to $4 to $5 a piece and sometimes only last a golfer a single hole.
300,000,000 golf balls are lost each year by Americans alone. It's also estimated that US golfers spend over $650 Million per year on those same golf balls.
I can't tell difference between snell and pro-v.
You don't need to play a premium golf ball.
The average golfer doesn't need to spend a ton of money on the high priced balls.
But on the real, someone of my skills doesn't need anything expensive but I like consistency.
When I get home from a round my wife doesn't ask me how I did. Instead she asks, 'how many balls did you lose.' That's no joke, she does.
I can accept hitting it deep in the woods or in the water, but knowing it's right there, somewhere in the tall grass, really aggravates me.
It has been estimated that the average golfer loses three to four balls a round.

Losing a golf ball on the course can be difficult to swallow, for a number of reasons, but it happens to us all from time to time. The best golf balls on the market are a worthwhile but often costly investment.
A dozen Titleist Pro V1 golf balls can set you back $35 a box, so losing that one begins to cut into your golf fun.
Losing a ball can not only cost you money, but it can ruin a great day out on the course.
I always thought balls made no difference. For years I just played whatever. I switched to Supersoft, and almost all my balls were 235+.
In my experience there is no difference in a 3 dollar ball and a 50 dollar ball.
Most golfers think they aren't good enough for a ProV or an expensive ball.
Until you get close to breaking 90, you don't need any better golf ball.
A beginner doesn't need a high spinning ball.
The assumption that a lesser golfer/higher handicap doesn't need premium balls... So how in the world could a high handicapper NOT benefit from using the right ball?
FACT: You don't need to spend $50 to get a premium ball.

A dozen Callaway Chrome Tour or Chrome Tour X golf balls, for instance, comes at an RRP of $55, which is $4.58 per ball. If you go through three balls per week, you're looking at an annual spend of $714.
I figured I would see a dropoff in performance, but honestly I didn't notice much difference at all.
Stop searching for the same golf ball Tiger Woods or Nelly Korda plays - it's probably hurting your game more than it's helping.
The truth is, most high handicappers are playing with the wrong balls - ones that actually exaggerate their mistakes rather than helping correct them.
Unless, like me, you find more than you lose... Then it becomes extremely cost effective!
The Kirkland ball is much cheaper, which makes it a solid choice for the average player.

My recommendation is to buy a big load of used balls. A 2-piece ball will do just fine. Many high handicappers lose a lot of balls, so buying in bulk should give you lots of balls to hit into the water and woods.

If you are truly a rank beginner, I'd say it is a waste of money. I don't think it will hurt you, but you won't get the benefit of the extra technology until your swing is fairly consistent from shot to shot.
Do you also hate losing expensive golf balls? We've set up a helpline.

For a brief shining moment back in 2016, the original Kirkland signature had its shot. Our own testing showed that very first K-Sig had what it took to challenge the Pro V1, particularly at $30 per double-dozen.
I couldn't tell any difference in performance from a ProV. They do seem to have a little bit of a 'clickier' sound when struck, but saw no decrease in distance or performance around the green.
It's not going to make you play any better, but it's your money to spend.
